pattern: A rectangle's left and right edges can only be a column's two points that sit right next to each other in that column, so anchor on adjacent pairs and sweep outward until something blocks the view.
hints: In any valid rectangle, the two points sharing an x-coordinate must be adjacent in that column's sorted y-order -- otherwise a third point of that column would sit on the edge between them.

Starting from such an adjacent pair, walk through the other columns left to right: a column with a point strictly between the two y-values kills every rectangle further out, so you can stop looking past it.

A column that contains both of the two y-values (and nothing strictly between them) completes a valid rectangle -- record its area, but you still cannot look past that column, since even one of the two y-values alone would end up sitting on a border of anything wider.

edge_inputs.description: fewer than four points, no rectangle possible
exactly the four corners of a square
a fifth point sits exactly on the border of the only candidate rectangle
a fifth point sits strictly inside the only candidate rectangle
points at the extreme coordinate values
an obstructing column sits between two otherwise-matching columns
several columns share the same adjacent y-pair, widening the best rectangle

stress.description: many points on a moderate coordinate grid, exercising long column-to-column sweeps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(1200, size if size >= 1 else 1))
    pts = set()
    while len(pts) < n:
        pts.add((rng.randint(0, 2000), rng.randint(0, 2000)))
    pts = list(pts)
    xCoord = [p[0] for p in pts]
    yCoord = [p[1] for p in pts]
    return [xCoord, yCoord]

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case, typically much less
